data.title: Upslope Capital Q1 2026 Investor Letter
data.description:
Dear Fellow Investor,

Upslope's objective is to deliver attractive, equity-like returns with significantly reduced market risk and low correlation versus traditional equity strategies. Q1 was a period of strong relative and absolute performance for Upslope - albeit with elevated up- and down-side volatility.

Market Conditions - a Strange Game

Early Q1 saw a sharp snapback in relative performance of defensive stocks. Ironically, by the time the war in Iran unfolded, defensives had run so hard that they sharply underperformed in a volatile macro environment typically conducive to good performance. While implications of the war are serious on a human and macroeconomic level, "serious" is not the right word to describe how financial markets processed events. Investors seemed far more interested in "Tweet trading" than any traditional fundamental analysis.

When it comes to navigating big macro events - e.g. wars, pandemics, recessions - Upslope's process is simple. First, determine whether the event is truly material or short-term noise. If the former, the next step is to quickly identify offside positioning in the portfolio. This means reducing outsized exposures to key risks - not eliminating them. The goal isn't to bet on a specific outcome (e.g. quick end to the war or a drawn-out conflict), but to move into balance so that the next breaking development or market-moving Tweet doesn't matter much to the portfolio. While paying close attention to big picture shifts is essential, predicting and acting on daily news flow and associated ups/downs in markets is not Upslope's game. "The only move is not to play."

As is typical during stretches of heightened volatility, Upslope made quite a few adjustments to the portfolio in Q1. Exited positions mostly fell into two categories: theses that had already played out (e.g. Sandvik) and sales aimed at concentrating the portfolio into higher conviction ideas with overlapping exposure (e.g. exiting Japan Exchange while holding CME and MarketAxess). Over time, this approach has evolved into more of a mainstay of Upslope's strategy: purchase a somewhat related group of stocks and narrow them down to fewer, higher-conviction positions over time. More details on these changes and new additions to the portfolio are below.

Portfolio Positioning

As of this writing, gross and beta-adjusted net exposures were 159% and 40%, respectively. Positioning reflects a high number of perceived opportunities on both (long + short) sides of the portfolio. Gross is elevated above the Fund's typical 150% ceiling due to hedged positions in Alphabet (GOOGL) and Intel.

Key Exits

The Fund exited Sandvik (SAND-SE, Sweden-based provider of mining, metal-cutting, and other industrial equipment and tools) and Holcim (HOLN-CH, Switzerland-based, global cement, aggregates and building materials company) due to full valuations and theses generally having played out.

The Fund also exited Japan Exchange Group (8697-JP, largest financial exchange operator in Japan), Booz Allen (BAH) (BAH, defense/security consulting firm), and Bio-Rad (BIO) (BIO, clinical diagnostic and life sciences products). While each situation was unique, the broader theme was one of concentrating the portfolio on other existing ideas and "battles." For Booz, the stock has come under pressure due to AI worries and political risk. While not the only portfolio company exposed to AI risk, Booz was the lowest conviction holding exposed to it and exiting enabled Upslope to add to higher conviction positions (e.g. FTI Consulting).

New Longs

As I've done in the past during unusually active quarters, new longs are presented in a bullet-pointed summary table below.

One supplemental comment: Upslope added three positions that I will awkwardly (because I haven't come up with a better term) refer to as "defensive-plus" businesses. These are diverse, well-managed businesses with large, highly defensive cores complemented by cyclical business units operating in trough-like conditions. This feels like the right market environment to own businesses like these, as they are unusually well positioned to take advantage of macro volatility. Most are also too boring for the current crop of investors to care much about today.

Somewhat by coincidence, each of the three "defensive-plus" additions are headquartered outside the United States (Canada, Sweden, and Finland - apparently Colorado's lack of winter this year is affecting me). In my view, the European additions have loosely similar exposure to the European stocks Upslope exited in Q1 - but with far less demanding valuations.

Exhibit 4: New Long Positions

Description Key Investment Points & Risks
Kesko (KESKOB-FI)

Kesko is the #2 grocer in Finland with nearly 35% market share (contributes 65% of company op profit). It also operates a building products business (26% of op profit) and a smaller Volkswagen-focused auto dealer network. The building products unit is geographically diverse (mostly across the Nordics) and has been acquisitive through the current down-cycle in housing.
  • ✓ Large market share (30-40%+ in key business units) and clean competitive advantages (e.g. unique entrepreneurial operating model) across segments
  • ✓ Operating structure enables it to effectively "fund" more aggressive expansion and consolidation in a challenged economic cycle while competitors retrench (i.e. Grocery unit ballast enables expansion in building products)
  • ✓ Trough-like macro conditions in building products, which could potentially double operating profit in the years ahead
  • ✓ Shares trade near middle of historical valuation range (16x EPS vs. 12-22x) - but on earnings currently depressed in Building and Technical Trade
  • ✗ Competition from discount grocer peers (e.g. Lidl)
  • ✗ Continued/extended macro challenges in cyclical segments
  • ✗ Government regulation (specifically focused on grocer sector)
  • ✗ Acquisition strategy (+ moderate leverage) could falter
Stella-Jones (SJ-CA)

Stella-Jones is a leading N. American manufacturer of specialty wood products for infrastructure. Products are primarily for utility poles (~50% of sales), rail ties (25%), and residential/other (25%). While headquartered in Montreal, 70% of sales are from the US with the remainder from Canada.
  • ✓ Dominant market share in rational/consolidated markets
  • ✓ Steady or better secular demand drivers (e.g. aging grid and infrastructure upgrades)
  • ✓ Strong capital allocation track record - disciplined M&A plus active and aggressive buyback program, while maintaining healthy balance sheet
  • ✓ Attractive valuation (13x EPS vs. 10-20x historically)
  • ✗ Potential for a rise in alternative material (metal) use for utility poles
  • ✗ Further insourcing of rail tie operations
  • ✗ Cyclicity in demand for rail ties, residential/other operations and, to a less correlated extent, utility poles
  • ✗ FX: sizable part of operations and cost base is Canadian
Lifco (LIFCO.B-SE)

Lifco is a Sweden-based serial acquiror diversified across Dental (20% of EBITA), Demolition & Tools (26% - niche machinery and attachments), and Systems Solutions (53% - specialty equipment, systems and manufacturing). The business is geographically diversified, but mostly (82%) focused on Europe.
  • ✓ Serial acquiror with defensive core and diversified cyclical exposure
  • ✓ Shares have de-rated (40x to 30x EPS) and are reasonable considering solid growth (historically 5-10% organic plus 5-10% acquired), strong cash flows/profitability (100% FCF conversion from EPS) and balance sheet (1.0x net leverage)
  • ✓ Cyclical units currently facing macro headwinds and should broadly benefit from eventual resolution of war in Ukraine
  • ✗ Serial acquiror strategy can falter - especially at large scale
  • ✗ Founder influence/control and uncertainty around succession
  • ✗ FX: largely based in Europe
  • ✗ Some overlap/correlation with existing Upslope positions (DPLM-LON + HSIC)
Jack Henry (JKHY)

Based in Missouri, Jack Henry is a leading provider of core processing and payments technology to banks across the U.S. The company is particularly strong and dominant in serving regional banks and credit unions.
  • ✓ Highly defensive, recurring revenue-based (90%) model - products are vital to the operations of JKHY's customers and extremely challenging to swap out
  • ✓ Strong capital allocation record - disciplined/selective M&A, opportunistic buyback and balance sheet that's effectively debt-free today
  • ✓ Sharply de-rated (40x to 20x EPS, 8x to 4x sales) largely due to recent "AI worries" among software businesses following a period of over-valuation
  • ✓ Recent margin inflection appears sustainable; estimates have continued to rise
  • ✗ AI risk - while Upslope's view is AI is more likely a benefit than a headwind, its future impact is impossible to know with certainty (and it will likely be large)
  • ✗ JKHY customer base continues to consolidate
  • ✗ General risks associated with a technology business that touches highly regulated financial services customers/activities (e.g. costly errors, cyber-attacks)

Source: Upslope, FactSet, company filings

Closing Thoughts

Geopolitics-driven volatility seems likely to remain unusually high for the years ahead. While it's impossible to predict the short term, this environment should continue to surface opportunities for those seeking high-quality businesses at attractive valuations. On the short side of the portfolio, actionable ideas abound.

Thank you for the trust you've placed in me and Upslope to manage a portion of your hard-earned money. Please contact me if you have any questions at all, would like to add to your investment, or know a qualified investor who may be a good fit for Upslope's unique approach.

Sincerely,

George K. Livadas

Appendix B: Portfolio Company (Long) Descriptions 4

CME Group (CME) : Leading operator of global derivatives exchanges; focused on rates, commodities, equities and FX. Attractive, durable business that should continue to benefit from global macro volatility. Competitive threat from upstart exchange continues to dissipate.

Crown Holdings (CCK) : One of the leading global aluminum beverage can producers, with strong geographic diversification. Decade-low leverage and EV multiples provide an attractive entry point for a company operating well in an out of favor staples-like sector.

Diploma (DPMAY) : U.K.-based specialty distributor focused on essential consumable products across life sciences, seals (machinery), and controls (aerospace wiring/harnesses). Unique model and conservative M&A strategy have historically enabled attractive free cash flow growth through the cycle.

Fanuc (FANUY), (6954-JP) : Leading global factory automation business exposed to reshoring and physical AI tailwinds. Reasonable valuation and undemanding consensus outlook. Position is highly complementary to Upslope's long and short holdings.

FTI Consulting (FCN) : Boutique consulting and advisory firm, with deep experience in restructuring, dispute/conflict advisory, and other practices. Challenges in a non-core segment have depressed expectations for shares despite an environment that should ultimately lead to strong and rising demand.

Henry Schein (HSIC) : Largest national distributor of dental products, as well as general healthcare supplies. Recent investment from KKR, along with long-time CEO transition, appears likely to accelerate efficiency improvements and revenue growth, while maintaining take-private optionality.

Jack Henry (JKHY) : Niche fintech business focused on core processing and payments, with dominant and entrenched market position among regional banks and credit unions. Steady/accelerating fundamentals in the face of AI headline noise delivered an opportunity to own shares in a growing and defensive company at an attractive price.

MarketAxess (MKTX) : Challenged member of fixed income/credit "exchange" duopoly. Given macro/geopolitical backdrop and depressed valuation, company appears likely to stabilize market share (or better) in the near-term and/or be acquired by a large exchange in the medium-term.

Kesko (KKOYF) (KESKOB-FI) : Uniquely diversified business spanning grocery, building products and auto dealers. Large, defensive grocery business (#2 share in consolidated Finnish market) enables management to allocate capital more aggressively during regional cyclical downturn in building products, as some competitors retrench.

Korea Defense Industry ETF (KDEF) : Concentrated ETF with exposure to South Korea's leading defense and shipbuilding contractors. Beneficiary of elevated/rising geopolitical tensions significant cost advantages vs. US/Eur peers, heightened policy uncertainty, and government-backed pro-shareholder/-market reforms.

Lifco (LFABF) (LIFCO.B-SE) : Storied serial acquiror that has de-rated in the face of various European macro headwinds. Valuation has become reasonable considering the company's track record of solid organic growth, supplemented by consistent and disciplined acquisition growth funded via strong free cash flow.

Smiths Group (SMGZY) (SMIN-LON) : Historically sleepy UK-based industrial conglomerate focused on specialty industrial niches and, previously, security/detection. Activist pressure led management to transform the business by shedding non-core units and focusing on the strongest legacy segments while aggressively returning capital.

Stella-Jones (SJ:CA) (STLJF) (STJNY) : Leading producer of specialty wood products (e.g. utility poles and rail ties) in North America with strong capital allocation track record and secular tailwinds stemming from aging infrastructure. Short-term challenges (e.g. rail tie insourcing) have created an opportunity to own shares at an attractive price.

STERIS (STE) : Leader in infection prevention, sterilization, and procedural solutions for healthcare providers, hospitals, pharmaceutical & bioprocess customers, and medical-device makers. Strong historic organic growth, despite majority of sales from recurring services and consumables.

Teledyne Tech. (TDY) : Producer of specialty electronics, components, and technologies - primarily focused on imaging sensors and cameras. Dinged by transformative 2021 FLIR acquisition, expectations and valuation reset, balance sheet is clean, and the company is positively exposed to rising defense spending and reshoring.

Appendix C: Terminology

Core Longs : Higher "quality" businesses (defined as low cyclicality, clean balance sheet, obvious and durable competitive advantages) managed with less valuation sensitivity (i.e. typically won't exit a Core long solely because of valuation) and assuming a multi-year time horizon.

Tactical Longs : Traditional "value" investments of more modest quality (reasonably or very cyclical, currently experiencing operational, competitive or financial challenges) managed with greater valuation sensitivity (i.e. typically begin to exit if shares approach full valuation) and assuming a shorter time horizon (typically 6-18 months).

Starter Longs : Generally smaller, undisclosed longs that fall into at least one of the following groups:

1. Ideas where I've completed enough work to establish a toehold, but still have more to do.
2. Companies facing obvious short-term challenges where I believe the near-term path in shares is likely lower, but the stock is 'cheap enough' and outright attractive over the longer-term.
3. 'Counter-shorts' - higher-beta longs with good risk/reward that trade more in-sync with Upslope's shorts on a daily basis and can be used to manage overall net long exposure more effectively and tax-efficiently.

Alpha Shorts : Individual company short positions.

Hedges : Diversified index hedges (either long or short).

data.title: Synchrony Financial: The Consumer Is Holding On - So Should Investors
data.description:
Q1 Results Show Steady Performance

Synchrony Financial (SYF) reported solid Q1 results, with no signs of consumer stress. Credit quality metrics were steady while purchase volume showed a healthy increase. Earnings outlook for the year was unchanged, however, as loan balances did not grow as much due to consumers paying off their cards more quickly than in prior cycles. The full year EPS guidance remains $9.10 - $9.50, which is also little changed from the 2025 actual result of $9.38.

This justified the downgrade to Hold in my January article. The stock has returned about 5% since then, but better buying opportunities were available during the market correction at the start of Iran hostilities in the first half of March. For the rest of the year, Synchrony expects to get back to growing loan balances due to purchase volume growth and some new card programs. On credit quality, delinquencies are now steadying after a period of declines, but charge-offs are now expected to be below the bottom of prior guidance. These benefits will be partially offset by Synchrony's arrangements with retail partners to share the card program profits. The company is also seeing cost growth mainly from IT expenses even as employee cost increases are minimal. This should pay off in improved efficiency in future years.

Overall, the share price and 2026 outlook have not changed enough to change my Hold rating, but the company's demonstrated success at avoiding consumer credit risk in a volatile environment, expected purchase volume growth, and technology investments driving efficiency all make Synchrony a good long-term investment. The company's continued substantial share count reduction through buybacks is another factor supporting the common shares. Investors desiring more income also have good choices in the preferred shares with either a fixed or 5-year resettable rate available to fit their interest rate outlook.

Consumer Purchasing Remains Solid

Synchrony has not had much cause to talk about the clichéd "K-shaped economy" you hear about so often from politicians, economists, and macro-focused analysts. If I had to assign the economy a letter based on Synchrony's various consumer platforms, I would call it M-shaped. The low and the high end both appear to be growing their spending while the middle remains subdued.

The smaller-ticket items generally purchased in the Diversified & Value and Digital platforms experienced 8-9% year on year purchase volume growth in Q1. The discretionary big-ticket items within the Lifestyle platform, such as motorcycles and other powersports equipment, also had strong purchase volume growth at 7%, although loan receivables growth was lower. These consumers paid off their balances quicker, possibly due to tax refunds in the quarter. It was Home & Auto with flat purchase volume growth and declining loan balances that was the biggest drag on the company in Q1. Still, this segment remains a key focus for new program growth, with the Lowe's (LOW) and Bob's Discount Furniture (BOBS) partnerships I discussed last quarter, along with new deals such as luxury home furnishings seller RH. For the full year, Synchrony is still expecting 5% loan receivables growth compared to the end of 2025.

Credit Quality Improvements Tail Off But Remain Solid

Synchrony's credit quality metrics have steadily improved over the last couple years, but that improvement is starting to flatten out based on 30- and 90-day delinquency rates. Both are almost identical to the rates seen 1 year ago. This is a sign that future improvements in charge-offs and the allowance for credit losses will slow down, but these metrics are currently at healthy levels. The charge-off rate was 5.42% in Q1, nearly 1 percentage point better than a year ago, and the allowance for credit losses was 10.42%, or 45 basis points better than last year.

For the full year, Synchrony is now forecasting a charge-off rate below 5.5%, an improvement from the 5.5-6.0% range projected at the start of this year. Some of this benefit from lower charge-offs is shared with Synchrony's retail partners through RSAs, or Retailer Sharing Arrangements, although RSAs as a percentage of receivables is still forecasted at 4.0-4.5% in 2026.

Operating Expense Growth Is An Investment In Future Efficiency

Operating Expenses grew 6% in Q1, although the biggest component, employee costs, grew just 2%. The big cost driver for the quarter was Information Processing, up 20%. This category includes fees paid to the credit card platforms like Mastercard (MA) but also covers spending on technology to improve the business. On the earnings call, we heard about some uses of AI at the company. On the cost saving side, Synchrony is using AI for coding applications and other functions to keep headcount growth flat. On the revenue enhancement side, Synchrony is working with its retail partners and tech companies to ensure that as agentic AI begins making purchases on behalf of consumers, Synchrony is embedded as a payment option.

For 2026, Synchrony is expecting operating expenses to grow in line with receivables, excluding a one-time $98 million cost last year.

Model Results and Valuation

In addition to the assumptions discussed above, I am revising down my net interest margin expectation for 2026 to 15.5%, in line with the actual Q1 result. This is below my previous estimate of 16.16% due to fewer projected interest rate cuts which would have lowered the cost of deposits. I also slightly increased loss reserves to 10.25% of receivables from 10.1% last quarter.

For 2026, I am forecasting EPS of $9.29, just $0.07 below my estimate last quarter and about a 1% decline from 2025 actuals. At a share price of $78.20, the forward P/E estimate is 8.4, about intermediate between year-end 2024 and 2025 levels.

On the balance sheet, I am assuming buybacks of $900 million per quarter, similar to Q1 levels. Synchrony has changed its buyback policy so that it no longer has an end date. The current buyback authorization recently approved by the board is for $6.5 billion. Still, the company noted on the earnings call they plan to keep buying back shares at a pace similar to recent quarters. It would take about 2 years to work through the current buyback authorization at the current pace.

Synchrony also announced that after a fifth quarterly dividend of $0.30 to be paid in May, they will be raising it to $0.34 starting in August. This 13.3% dividend raise results in a forward yield of about 1.7%.

With this capital return, I am projecting Synchrony to end 2026 with a book value of $48.09 and tangible book value of $39.83. That puts P/B around 1.63 and P/TB at 1.96, cheaper than where it ended 2025 but about in line with year-end 2024 levels.

Synchrony remains well-capitalized as I project year-end common equity / asset ratio to be 11.3%. This is lower than recent years as the bank finally starts growing loan balances again, but it is not too low to be a concern. The CFO mentioned on the earnings call an interest in issuing more preferred shares to increase Tier 1 leverage ratio but did not consider that metric a constraint today.

Looking at the Seeking Alpha Quant Rankings, Synchrony is little changed from last quarter, with a Hold rating and score of 3.16. That places it 19th in the Consumer Finance industry, just under closest peers Ally (ALLY) at 18th and Capital One (COF) at 17th. Synchrony's main shortfall vs. these competitors is in the Growth category with a grade of C-, compared to A for Ally and A- for Capital One. The Growth grade for Synchrony should improve if the company delivers on its plan to increase loan receivables by 5% in 2026. These balances have been flat to down the last couple of years as new program growth has been subdued and customers have been paying off their accounts more quickly than in previous years.

Update on Preferred Shares

Synchrony has two classes of preferred shares. Series A (SYF.PR.A) was issued in 2019 in a lower interest rate environment and pays a fixed coupon rate of 5.625% based on a par value of $25. At a market price of $20.00, the current yield is 7.0%. It is now callable on any payment date, but given the current interest rate environment, a call is highly unlikely. Series B (SYF.PR.B) was issued in 2024 and pays a coupon rate of 8.25% but will reset in 2029 and every 5 years thereafter based on the 5-year US Treasury note yield (US5Y) plus 4.044%. At a market price of $26.20, the current yield is 7.9%. Series B is callable at each reset date. If interest rates are lower in 2029, and the market is comfortable with Synchrony's credit quality, there is some chance of Series B getting called at $25.00, creating a capital loss from the current price. However, I believe this risk is low with Synchrony indicating they are considering issuing more preferred stock. If the 5-year Treasury is still yielding the current rate of 3.93% in 2029, Series B would see a dividend cut to a 7.97% coupon if not called.

Since my last article, Series A has come from behind to slightly outperform Series B, in line with my Buy rating on A and Hold rating on B. The spread of about 90 basis points between the current yields of the two preferreds is similar to last quarter.

The preferred dividends are well-covered. Total preferred dividend payout in 2025 was $83 million, compared to net income before preferred dividends of $3.55 billion. I would not expect the issuance of a new series of preferred shares to materially impact this high coverage.

Note that to avoid a dividend cut in 2029, the 5-year Treasury would have to be yielding more than 4.21% when the rate resets in February 2029. Yields have not exceeded this level since February 2025. The model from econforecasting.com shows the 5-year yield approaching or slightly exceeding this level at some points in the future, but not by much. The risk of a dividend cut for Series B if rates are lower, along with the risk of a call if rates are higher, is a continuing reason to avoid Series B. Series A has the risk of share price decline if rates increase but no risk of a dividend cut. If rates are falling as the Series B reset date approaches in 2029, the price of Series A is much more likely to benefit.

Conclusion

Synchrony common shares are only slightly above where they were trading at the time of my last article when I rated them a Hold, but better prices were available during the March market correction. Synchrony's lending standards, good relationships with retail partners, and use of technology have allowed the bank to continue delivering steady results amid macro worries about the health of the consumer. While earnings growth is still expected to be flat in 2026, the growth in loan balances from new card programs and higher purchase volume sets up the company for EPS growth in future years. EPS growth will also be helped by Synchrony's strong buyback program.

With valuations not expensive but not cheap compared to history, the common shares remain a Hold as the bank has proven itself to be a prudent lender and steward of investors' capital. Any dips into the $60s from general market unease would be buying opportunities.

While Synchrony has favored buybacks over high dividends for returning capital to common shareholders, investors who prefer more income would find Synchrony Series A preferred shares to be a good choice. The preferred dividends are well covered by earnings, including Series B and any likely future preferred issue being considered. Series A would be expected to outperform Series B and avoid being called in an interest rate environment that is flat to declining. Series B remains a Hold due to the risk of a dividend cut or a call at $25 in 2029.

data.title: Groupe Dynamite: Newest Canadian Retail Growth Story
data.description:
Groupe Dynamite (GRGD:CA) is an interesting story with a long history dating back to the 1970s and a recent uptick in popularity. The company is following in the footsteps of its larger competitor Aritzia (ATZ:CA), which has been a darling of the Toronto Stock Exchange for the past 5 years. After its late 2024 IPO, GRGD has shown very impressive growth with accelerating comparable store sales and a growing US presence. The equity has pulled back recently, marking an intriguing entry point for long-term investors looking for something with significant growth potential. At a sub-$10 billion market cap in Canadian dollars, the equity is a small cap, but the company has proven it can grow through a difficult retail cycle with solid long-term management.

Two Emerging Growth Brands - Garage and Dynamite

GRGD is leaning into its two unique retail store brands, Garage and Dynamite, with current US expansion focused on Garage. Garage is focused on the Gen Z late teens to 20s cohort, which is looking for trendy fashion at a reasonable price. Dynamite, focused more on the mid-30s cohort, is seeing repositioning and expansion but not the level of growth being seen at Garage into 2026. Garage is seeing all the new store additions for 2026 in the United States, as it follows in the footsteps of 2025 stalwart Aritzia with everyday luxury-style women's attire. Groupe Dynamite has exceptional margins for a clothing retailer at 60% plus, as they have been increasing prices at over twice the rate of inflation in recent years. This is leading to very strong comparable sales growth and allows management to lean into new store openings and renovations. Garage, in particular, is a hot brand right now, with youth flocking to the reasonably priced casual wear.

After 2019, the company led by Andrew Lufty has been on a more growth-focused trajectory, which you can see from the improving same-store sales below. Improving data on the customer base and a more professional management team have led the company to significantly improve the financial performance. This inflected in fiscal 2025 with strong margin expansion and same-store sales growth of 26.7% in the full year. Stores are paying back in less than two years, and the time to market for GRGD products is giving them a structural advantage. They are purchasing much of their product in-season, allowing them to capitalize on trends more than other brands. This also enables them to maximize their margins and reduce markdowns to a low level as they reduce product that is out of style for each season. This has proven very successful for them, and the power of US growth is now showing through strongly as well. The company still has significant untapped potential through both US store openings as well as international expansion. They have opened a store in London and will use that to learn about the European market for coming expansion. At a limited number of stores, GRGD has significant gains to be made in both awareness and spreading of their retail locations across the US as well.

The fiscal fourth quarter, just reported on April 1st, was very strong, with comparable sales staying at an exceptional rate for the second quarter in a row at a stellar 30.4%. This led to 40.3% revenue growth in the quarter, with 43.3% growth in online sales. Online sales are a very small portion of sales at just 17.4% of total retail sales. Look for this number to grow faster than the store business and become a greater portion over time, helping to boost growth. Gross margins came in at 66.1% - a level higher than the most profitable clothing companies out there - showing strong purchasing and inventory discipline. This led to a phenomenal increase in earnings of 75% year over year, as operating income was up 90% y/y to $120.3 million. At a 33% operating margin, the company has impressive structural margins considering the currently difficult operating environment most of its peers are facing. The company only has $183m left of net debt, leading to a net cash position within a year. This will allow the company to continue to redeploy cash to shareholders through buybacks or accelerate growth plans for international expansion. While the company guided to 11-14% comparable sales growth for the year, this number is likely to be revised higher with good execution. A number above 15% would support a continued earnings multiple in the high 20% to low 30% range, depending on operating margin. The 35x earnings multiple it reached in December will be difficult to reach with growth slowing from here due to difficult comparisons.

The unique inventory management capabilities have propelled the US market growth with 131 stores in the US. They are adding a large amount of new Garage stores in the US, with Dynamite focused predominantly in Canada. They are focused on high-end locations and plan to actually close some of the weaker locations to upscale the offering. Above you can see that while the most recent year of same-store sales growth may decelerate to a more reasonable 10%, the company has solid upside from there. The US market continues to prove lucrative, allowing for share improvement even with high expectations. Below you can see that shares are off from the highs to a reasonable 29x forward earnings. Growth will be slower in 2026 than in 2025, but operating margins for the full year will be higher as scale continues to increase. Estimates are starting at a higher point than last year but still have good potential to move up from the 22% level currently closer to 30%.

On April 20, CEO Andrew Lufty disclosed he was selling $251 million in shares through his holding company, reducing his stake several percent. However, he still owns 81.9% of the shares, making him extremely aligned with shareholders for long-term value creation. These continued sales that will happen from time to time will actually help GRGD become more accessible for money managers and funds, as it has very low liquidity as of now. Long term I see this as a positive, but it has put shares under pressure with an 11% drop on April 21st due to the optics of the transaction and increase in float size. This is a buying opportunity that I will use to add to my existing positions, as it has no impact on the long-term fundamental trajectory of the business.

Further 2026 Upside is Coming

GRGD has a long runway to grow, with the new UK stores just starting to contribute and a modest foothold in the US with Garage. The plan is for a slow and measured move into Europe, increasing profitability and growth with minimal costs. I see forward multiple remaining in the high 20s to low 30s depending on the next few quarters, supporting another strong 2026 result. Estimates are continuing to improve as growth stays underappreciated with their nimble operating model. The earnings multiple will likely not expand much higher beyond 30 as growth slows, but steady growth and operating margin expansion should still have GRGD shares significantly outperforming the wider market in 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vicor: The Soaring Backlog Speaks Volumes
data.description:
I last covered Vicor Corporation (VICR) in October, and so it's time for an update. Back then, I expressed my view that the narrative had changed, and as a result, I double upgraded the stock from a sell to a buy rating. I cited factors including accelerating revenues, expanding margins, and a P/E that was below its five-year average. As you can see in the chart below, turning bullish has paid off handsomely, as Vicor has soared over 190% since the publication of that article. The company reported their most recent earnings just earlier this morning (April 21st), and so today I will be taking a deep dive into their results.

Below, it is shown that Vicor's Q1 was a quarter of outperformance. Top line growth accelerated, and margins expanded. However, most importantly, their backlog has swelled, and the company is aiming to increase capacity to help boost their financial growth. While IP infringement against the company presents some risks and the valuation isn't cheap, I believe the increasingly strong growth story justifies a reiteration of the buy rating.

A Critical Infrastructure Provider

Before we get into their 2026 Q1, let's take a look at the big picture to provide a sense of how the company is positioned. As you can see above, the company is involved in four main markets, and so their business is quite diversified in terms of end use. However, in my view, the largest opportunities moving forward will likely be in HPC and Aerospace & Defense. Firstly, HPC needs no introduction. With the AI infrastructure buildout showing little signs of slowing down, Vicor is likely to continue benefitting from the tailwinds.

Secondly, with the space race heating up and geopolitical tensions flaring up lately, their Aerospace and Defense business is positioned to deliver strong growth in my view. For example, Vicor is involved with missile defense systems and unmanned systems. With the world seemingly becoming an increasingly dangerous place with the Ukraine war and Iran war ongoing, I believe Vicor should see tailwinds here as well. Instead of just being a power components and power systems provider, they have now positioned themselves as a critical infrastructure company.

Lastly, you can see above that their SAM is projected to expand significantly as we move towards 2028. Note that for 2025, Vicor generated a total of $408 million in product and royalty revenue, and so the company has a lot of room to grow over the next few years.

Demand is Robust

For 2026 Q1, there were strong signs that demand continues to strengthen for Vicor. For the top line, the company generated $113 million in product and royalty revenues, up 20.2% YoY. This is a notable acceleration from Q4's 15.3% increase, and so business activity is accelerating. It should also be mentioned that they beat top line expectations by a fair margin of $3.65 million, and so Q1 was a quarter of outperformance.

While the acceleration in revenue growth already gives us a good clue as to where demand is, the backlog numbers really speak volumes. At the end of Q1, backlog was $301 million, up 75% YoY and 70% QoQ. CEO Dr. Patrizio Vinciarelli stated that

Rising demand across high-performance compute, automatic test equipment, and industrial, aerospace and defense applications is reflected in a 70% sequential increase in backlog, setting the stage for revenue growth.

Back in Q4, there was just a 13.8% YoY and 15.8% QoQ increase in backlog, and so it is clear that demand has strengthened significantly in recent times. While Vicor did not provide forward looking guidance, I believe it wouldn't be unreasonable to expect further revenue growth acceleration moving forward with demand being so strong.

Margins Expand

With demand being so robust, it is not a major surprise that Vicor's gross margin expanded from 47.2% in the year ago quarter to 55.2%. At this point, it seems that their pricing power is strong, as they are providing innovative products that offer value to customers. Meanwhile, the company's internal efficiency is also on the right track. For operating expenses, Vicor saw a very mild increase YoY from $44.5 million to just $45.5 million. Considering where the top line growth rate is, this is a demonstration of strong expense control, and this implies that operating margin saw improvement as well.

As for the bottom line, there has been clear improvement. With revenue growth accelerating and margins expanding, Vicor reported a diluted EPS of $0.44, up sharply from $0.06 in the prior year period. Note that they beat EPS expectations rather comfortably, as they surpassed estimates by $0.11. In percentage terms, the bottom line beat significantly more than the top line did, and so the profitability of their business is showing outperformance as well.

Capacity Expansion

With the backlog growing faster than the top line, I would say that their capacity expansion is both logical and justified. In their Q1 earnings release, they stated that

We are expanding capacity with additional equipment in our first CHiP fab while planning a second fab. Expanding total capacity with a second fab and an alternate source of high current density 2nd Gen VPD modules will give OEMs and Hyper-scalers redundant access to enabling VPD power system technology.

While strong demand showing up in the form of a huge backlog is a promising sign, at the end of the day the company obviously needs to effectively monetize this in order for their earnings to really benefit. As capacity at their first fab increases and their second fab progresses, investors can expect the realization of revenues to become increasingly evident.

Risks

Royalties are a significant part of Vicor's business, and so the supposed IP infringement that has been going on has taken some steam out of their business. In their earnings release, they stated that

Precluding unlawful importation of computing systems infringing Vicor IP is having an effect. The industry is learning to pay attention to the multiplicity of innovations pioneered by Vicor and the need for a license to avoid disruption of supply from copycat power system manufacturers.

If not for these infringements, growth for Vicor could potentially have been even higher, and so they are obviously taking legal action. Do note, however, that in their earnings call, CFO Jim Schmidt stated that they saw "higher legal expenses related to enforcement of [their] IP." As a result, there are also some risks to their near term profitability.

Valuation Has Soared

Since my previous article, there has been significant expansion in Vicor's valuation multiples. For my preferred measure, the non-GAAP forward P/E, there has been a jump from 42.04 to 84.41. While this multiple was already at a premium to the sector back in October, it was still below its own five year average. As you can see above, it is now 43.42% above that average. In my view, while the valuation has soared, there are still enough signals that show the risk/reward remains attractive for investors.

Today, Q1 was shown to be a robust quarter for a variety of metrics. Revenue growth accelerated, the backlog is surging, and margins are also expanding significantly. With HPC tailwinds likely to continue moving forward and the SAM being sizeable, opportunity for growth remains immense. Furthermore, the capacity expansion plans will help them to capture more of this opportunity, and so overall I would say that the outlook has grown stronger.

There is no question that the valuation has gotten a little expensive, but it is important to note that the company is increasingly becoming a critical infrastructure provider. Vicor's solutions are valuable for high stakes sectors, including national security, and so I believe that a premium valuation is justified given the fundamentals and their prospects.

Conclusion

Vicor has experienced monstrous gains since my previous article. The stock is up over 190%, but I believe there are still enough reasons for investors to continue buying. In recent times, the company has shifted from being a relatively 'boring' power component and power systems provider into a company that supports the AI revolution and advanced military solutions. Vicor now has the financials to back up this change in narrative. While the valuation is not cheap by any means, the opportunity for the company and their strong execution make the risk/reward still favorable for investors in my view. Therefore, I've decided to reiterate my buy rating on Vicor.

data.title: Did Americans Scrimp At Other Retailers To Buy Gas? Nope, Not Americans. Born To Splurge
data.description:
Even without gas stations, retail sales jumped, powered by e-commerce, auto dealers, general merchandise stores, and other retailers.

Retail sales soared in March by 1.7% from February, seasonally adjusted. Compared to March last year, sales jumped by 4.5%, not seasonally adjusted. But there was this spike in the price of gasoline, which caused sales at gas stations to spike.

So the question arises: Did Americans cut spending on other stuff that they buy at retailers in order to buy gasoline? Nope, not Americans. Born to splurge.

Retail sales without gas stations jumped by 0.61% in March from February, after having already jumped by 0.67% in February, seasonally adjusted (red line in the chart). Year-over-year, retail sales without gas stations rose by 3.5% not seasonally adjusted (blue).

Avid inflation-adjusters, note: Most inflation is in services, and retailers don’t sell services; they sell goods. CPI inflation in durable goods was close to 0% in March, both month-to-month and year-over-year. And CPI inflation in food bought at grocers was 0% in March and less than 2% year-over-year. The CPI for gasoline spiked, and that explains the spike in gasoline sales; see the chart further below.

Inflation in services, which is where Americans do two-thirds of their spending, was over 3%. But you cannot adjust retail sales to inflation in services that retailers don’t sell. You have to adjust retail sales to inflation in the goods that retailers sell. And outside of gasoline, there wasn’t much.

The largest categories of retailers.

E-commerce sales (18% of total retail sales) have been soaring relentlessly, gobbling up market share from brick-and-mortar retailers, growing year-over-year at over twice the rate of overall retail sales.

This includes e-commerce sales by retailers with brick-and-mortar stores, such as Walmart (WMT), Macy’s (M), Costco (COST), etc. Their online sales count here, and their brick-and-mortar sales count in their brick-and-mortar category.

In March, sales at these nonstore retailers jumped by 1.0% from February, seasonally adjusted, to a record $135 billion, making it the #1 largest category of retailers in March.

Year-over-year, sales jumped by 13% not seasonally adjusted and by 10% seasonally adjusted. There is no sign that consumers cut their online spending because they spent more on gasoline. These are big sales increases, even for ecommerce:

Sales at Auto & other motor vehicle dealers (17% of total retail sales) rose by 0.6% in March from February, seasonally adjusted. Year-over-year, sales fell by 2.8% not seasonally adjusted (blue line). But that was compared to March 2025, when tariff-fearmongering clickbait in the mainstream media, financial media, and social media about exploding auto prices caused a massive wave of frontrunning and a huge spike in sales.

Those prices ended up barely budging, as automakers had to eat all of the tariffs to not lose sales and market share. Beyond that front-running-distorted comparison, sales in March this year were pretty good, the second-highest ever, up by 18% month-to-month not seasonally adjusted, and by 0.6% seasonally adjusted.

Sales at Food services & drinking places, such as restaurants and bars (12% of total retail sales), rose by 0.1% in March from February, seasonally adjusted. Year-over-year, sales rose by 2.4% seasonally adjusted and by 1.6% not seasonally adjusted.

Food & Beverage stores, such as brick-and-mortar supermarkets, grocery stores, and specialized beverage stores (12% of total retail sales) have long been losing market share to online grocery retailers, including their own online operations, to “general merchandise stores,” such as Walmart and Costco, that do a booming business in groceries, and to restaurants (see chart above).

So when “sales at food & beverage stores” languish, it’s not because Americans went on a diet, but because sales shifted to other types of retailers, including ecommerce, general merchandise stores, and restaurants. This has been going on for many years.

Walmart, the largest grocer in the US, has been aggressively going after the grocery business, including the ecommerce grocery business, none of which count here in this category. Amazon (AMZN) has been selling groceries online in various iterations.

There is now a huge choice of online food retailers – both online-only retailers and the online operations of brick-and-mortar retailers. And these sales don’t count in this category of “Food & beverage stores”; they count in e-commerce, regardless of how the food got into the house, whether delivered by the company or a carrier, or picked up by the customer. And we can see this change show up here in the numbers.

Sales at food & beverage stores (brick-and-mortar) jumped 0.7% in March from February but were unchanged year-over-year, at $84 billion

Sales at General merchandise stores (11% of total retail sales) jumped by 1.0% in March from February to $79 billion. Year-over-year, sales were up by 3.0% not seasonally adjusted and by 2.5% seasonally adjusted.

This category includes Walmart, Costco, Target (TGT), and many others. Many of them also sell groceries, with Walmart being the #1 grocer in the US, and their grocery sales are here in this category, not in the category of food & beverage stores.

But sales of their e-commerce operations are included under the e-commerce retailers above.

Sales at gas stations (7% of total retail sales) move in lockstep with the price of gasoline. Gasoline prices spiked in March, so sales at gas stations spiked in March.

Sales at gas stations spiked by 15.4% in March from February, and by 18% year-over-year to $61 billion (red in the chart below, left scale). The purple dotted line (right scale) shows the CPI for gasoline, which also spiked.

Sales at Building materials, garden supply and equipment stores (6% of total retail sales) jumped by 0.7% in March from February, seasonally adjusted, to $42 billion. Year-over-year sales were up by 2.6% seasonally adjusted and by 5.3% not seasonally adjusted.

Sales at Health and personal care stores (5% of total retail sales) rose by 0.5% in March from February, seasonally adjusted, to $40 billion. Year-over-year, sales rose by 3.0% not seasonally adjusted and by 1.6% seasonally adjusted.

Original Post

data.title: Constellation Software: A Business Built To Compound
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Rowan Street Q1 2026 Letter.

During the quarter, we initiated a position in Constellation Software (CNSWF) (CNSWY) (CSU:CA). This decision reflects our continued effort to allocate capital toward what we believe are the highest-quality long-term compounders available to us.

We have been shareholders of Topicus (TOITF) (TOI:CA), a spin-off from Constellation Software, since 2021. Over this period, we have been highly impressed with its execution, capital allocation discipline, and ability to compound value through the acquisition of vertical market software (VMS) businesses.

Our familiarity with Topicus has provided us with a deeper understanding of the broader Constellation ecosystem-its decentralized operating model, disciplined approach to capital allocation, and the durability of its business model. In many ways, we view our investments in Constellation Software and Topicus as part of the same underlying system, sharing a common philosophy and approach to long-term value creation.

A Business Built to Compound

Constellation operates a simple but highly effective model. The company acquires small, mission-critical software businesses that serve specific industries-commonly referred to as vertical market software companies. These businesses provide essential systems that customers rely on to run their daily operations. While the cost of the software is relatively small, its importance is high, resulting in low customer churn and highly recurring revenues.

These markets are often too small to attract meaningful competition, yet large enough to support attractive economics. As a result, many of these businesses enjoy durable competitive positions within their niches.

What makes Constellation exceptional, however, is not just what it owns, but how it allocates capital. Since founding the company in 1995, Mark Leonard has built one of the most disciplined capital allocation platforms in the public markets. The formula is straightforward: acquire high-quality, cash-generative businesses at sensible prices - typically founder-led companies that are leaders in their respective niches - decentralize their operations, hold them forever, and systematically reinvest the cash they produce into acquiring more businesses just like them. Rather than absorbing acquisitions into a centralized structure, Constellation provides guidance, capital, and best practices while allowing local management teams to continue running their businesses independently. It is this reinvestment flywheel, repeated hundreds of times over three decades, that has driven Constellation's exceptional long-term compounding - delivering returns on invested capital above 20% for decades, a track record that very few businesses can match.

The Current Disconnect

Since its public listing in 2006, Constellation Software has compounded shareholder capital at an exceptional rate, generating a cumulative return of over 13,000%, equivalent to approximately 28% annually. Importantly, this long-term performance has been achieved despite the recent sharp decline in the share price.

As the chart below illustrates, the recent drawdown of approximately 50% is among the most severe in Constellation's public history. While the company has experienced periodic drawdowns over the past two decades-often in the 20-30% range-the current decline stands out both in magnitude and relative to prior periods.

However, when we examine the underlying business performance, the picture looks very different. In 2025, revenue grew approximately 15%, cash flow from operations increased by 24%, and free cash flow available to shareholders (FCFA2S) grew by 14%. Over the past decade, Constellation has compounded revenue at roughly 20% annually and free cash flow at approximately 21%. The disconnect between business performance and recent share price is particularly notable, as the company continues to execute at a high level despite the recent decline in its stock.

What Is Driving the Decline?

The recent weakness in Constellation's share price appears to be driven by two primary concerns.

1. Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Software

First is artificial intelligence and its potential to disrupt the software industry. More broadly, sentiment toward software companies has turned sharply negative, driven in part by the belief that advances in AI will commoditize software development-lowering barriers to entry and enabling customers to build their own solutions at a fraction of the cost.

While this may prove true in certain segments of the market, we believe this narrative is often applied too broadly and may not fully account for the structural differences between horizontal software and the vertical market software businesses that Constellation owns.

In many cases, these systems have been developed, refined, and embedded into customer workflows over decades. They are not simply tools, but integral components of how these businesses operate on a day-to-day basis. As a result, the switching costs are not merely financial-they are operational, and in some cases existential.

Importantly, the decision to replace or rebuild such systems is not driven solely by technological feasibility, but by organizational reality. For many of Constellation's customers-often small and mid-sized businesses-the risks associated with disrupting core systems may outweigh the potential cost savings from alternative solutions. Replacing these systems would require not only technical expertise, but also significant time, capital, and organizational change, all while introducing meaningful execution risk.

In addition, the value of the data embedded within these systems is often substantial. Over time, these platforms accumulate mission-critical customer, operational, and historical data, which can be difficult-if not impossible-to replicate without disruption.

As a result, we believe the risk of rapid displacement may be lower than current market sentiment implies. While AI will undoubtedly influence how software is developed and used over time, we believe it is more likely to enhance the capabilities of Constellation's vertical market software businesses-making them more valuable to their customers-rather than render them obsolete.

In our view, the market may be extrapolating a generalized narrative onto a highly specialized segment of the software ecosystem, without fully accounting for the durability of these business models.

Importantly, Constellation is not standing still in the face of these developments. Over the past several years, the company has been actively incorporating AI across its operating groups-investing in upskilling its development teams, integrating AI-assisted tools into workflows, and facilitating the sharing of best practices across its portfolio.

Under the leadership of Mark Miller-who has been with Constellation since its first acquisition in 1995, built much of its decentralized operating model alongside Mark Leonard-this is not a new direction, but a continuation of how the company has always operated. Constellation's approach to AI reflects its core philosophy: a decentralized organization with a strong learning culture, where individual business units apply new technologies in ways that are directly relevant to their customers and vertical markets, rather than through a top-down mandate.

Importantly, management has indicated that long-term differentiation will not come from simply building software faster, but from what these businesses have spent decades developing: deep domain expertise, embedded workflows, proprietary data, and trusted relationships. In this context, AI is more likely to enhance these advantages than erode them.

In addition, the current narrative around AI may create indirect opportunities for Constellation's capital allocation strategy. As uncertainty around software increases, valuations of potential acquisition targets may decline, which could allow the company to deploy capital at more attractive returns.

Taken together, we believe Constellation may be well positioned not only to navigate this transition, but potentially to benefit from it over time. In many ways, its decentralized structure and culture of continuous learning may prove to be a competitive advantage in adapting to technological change.

2. Leadership Transition

In September 2025, Mark Leonard resigned as President of Constellation for health reasons - a development that, understandably, rattled investors. Leonard is not just a founder; he is widely regarded as one of the greatest capital allocators of his generation. The market had long assigned a premium to his presence, and his departure raised legitimate questions about whether the culture and discipline he built could endure without him.

However, we believe this concern, while valid, underestimates the durability of what Leonard actually built. Constellation was never designed as a one-person organization. Its decentralized structure, deep bench of operators, and disciplined capital allocation framework were built precisely to function independently of any single individual - including its founder.

In a recent call with investors, Mark Miller was explicit: it is business as usual. No changes to strategy, no changes to the management team, and a continued focus on the same long-term, decentralized approach that has compounded capital for three decades.

Conclusion

Investing is rarely comfortable during periods of heightened uncertainty, and Constellation Software today is no exception. A 50% drawdown, concerns around AI disruption, and a leadership transition have created a level of skepticism that, in our view, is understandable. These are legitimate concerns that deserve serious consideration.

Investing, however, is a game of probabilities-not certainties. When we weigh the evidence, we believe the odds favor Constellation navigating these challenges successfully. Its decentralized structure, depth of management, and durable business model were built to withstand periods like this.

The challenge with businesses like Constellation is not understanding them-it is owning them through periods like this. Over time, business performance and market perception tend to converge-but rarely in a way that feels comfortable in the moment. In our experience, it is often these periods-when uncertainty is elevated but the underlying system remains intact-that matter most for long-term outcomes.

About Rowan Street Capital

Rowan Street Capital is a long-term, concentrated investment partnership founded in 2015 by Alex Kopel and Joe Maas. We are patient owners of a carefully selected group of exceptional businesses, investing alongside founder-operators and management teams who build durable value over time. We charge no fixed management fees - our compensation is tied directly to the performance of our partners' capital.

If our way of thinking resonates with you, we invite you to follow our work and explore our full investment philosophy at Rowan Street Capital.

data.title: Meta Platforms: The Long Game
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Rowan Street Q1 2026 Letter.

Meta (META) has delivered a cumulative return of approximately 1,300% since its IPO, or about 21% annually. The path to those returns, however, has been anything but smooth.

Over the past decade, the stock has experienced numerous drawdowns of 30% or more, several declines of 50% or more, and most notably, a decline of nearly 80% in 2022. These periods were not isolated events - they were a recurring feature of owning the business.

Everyone aspires to earn returns of this magnitude and describes themselves as a long-term investor. In practice, very few are willing - or able - to endure the level of volatility required to achieve them.

The Current Setup

Meta Platforms (META) is where we believe the current environment has created a compelling opportunity in our portfolio.

In late January, the company reported one of its strongest quarters in recent memory - revenues of $59.9 billion, growing 24% year over year, with earnings well ahead of expectations. The stock jumped nearly 10% on the news. Investors were gaining confidence that Meta's massive AI investment was offensive in nature - deepening its moat rather than straining its balance sheet.

Then two court rulings arrived in quick succession.

One in New Mexico, one in California, found Meta liable for failing to adequately protect young users on its platforms. A $375 million penalty was imposed, and the prospect of further litigation remains real. The stock lost over $100 billion in market value in a matter of days. Combined with broader tech weakness, Meta now sits approximately 12% below where it started the year - and nearly 30% below the highs it reached in the summer of 2025.

We will not minimize the legal risk. These are genuine headwinds, not footnotes. But Meta generated over $43 billion in free cash flow last year. For a business of this scale, these are manageable costs - serious, but not existential. In our view, the selloff was a reaction to headlines, not to business fundamentals.

We Have Seen This Before

In November 2022, with Meta's stock down 75% and its market cap having shed $750 billion in a single year, we published a piece asking a simple question: does this make any sense?

The answer, in our view, was no. The stock price had become disconnected from the underlying business. The market was focused on near-term cost increases and capital expenditures, while ignoring what Zuckerberg was actually building. We argued then that the spending was defensive and necessary, that the founder-CEO mindset made all the difference, and that the long-term earnings power of the business was being significantly underappreciated.

What followed was one of the great business re-ratings of the past decade.

We believe certain elements of that dynamic may be present today - though every situation is different and past outcomes are not a guide to future results. For those interested, the full 2022 piece is available on our website.

If this way of thinking resonates with you, you can subscribe below to follow our work.

The Concerns Are Real - But Familiar in Character

Meta has announced capital expenditures of up to $135 billion in 2026, nearly double what it spent in 2025. Operating margin has already compressed from 48% to 41% in the most recent quarter. The market's central concern is not that Meta is unprofitable today - it generated over $43 billion in free cash flow last year. The concern is whether $135 billion in annual capital expenditure will generate returns that justify the investment, and when. That uncertainty is real. We do not dismiss it.

But here is what the numbers actually show. Meta reaches over 3.5 billion people every single day across Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger. Its advertising engine grew revenues over 20% last year. And the AI systems it has been building are not speculative bets on some distant future - they are already working, quietly improving engagement, ad pricing, and monetization in the background.

What struck us most in the most recent earnings call was not the revenue growth - though it was impressive. It was the discipline behind the capital allocation. Management described a rigorous, ROI-driven process: projecting returns across ads performance, engagement, and AI efficiency initiatives, stack-ranking them, and funding only those with attractive expected returns. The 2025 investments delivered strong results. The same framework is being applied to 2026.

This is not reckless spending. This is a great business systematically reinvesting into high-return improvements to its own engine while building the infrastructure it believes will define the next era of AI-driven computing. The spending is offensive, not desperate. There is an important difference.

Where We Are Focused

In our view, the market today is focused on what Meta will earn in the next few quarters. We are focused on what it will look like in five to ten years. That difference in time horizon is, we believe, where the opportunity lives.

Zuckerberg is investing decisively in AI dominance - though it is worth being precise about where Meta stands today. In the race to build frontier AI models, Meta has faced setbacks. Its Avocado model was delayed after internal tests showed it trailing competitors from Google (GOOGL) and OpenAI. That is a real challenge and the market has noticed.

But here is a distinction worth emphasizing. Meta does not need to win the AI model race to win the AI era. It needs AI to make its existing products - used by over 3.5 billion people every day - more valuable. And on that front, the evidence is already compelling. AI-powered recommendation systems are improving engagement and ad pricing today. AI video tools alone are generating $10 billion in annual recurring revenue, growing three times faster than overall advertising revenue. The infrastructure being built now is designed to deepen these advantages further.

This is a business using AI as a weapon to compound an already dominant position - not a startup betting its survival on building the best model.

The people running this company have put that conviction in writing. In March 2026, Meta filed with the SEC a new executive compensation structure - the first stock option grants the company has issued since its 2012 IPO. Six of Meta's most senior leaders, including the CFO, CTO, and COO, were granted options that pay out only if Meta's stock reaches $3,727 per share by 2031 - implying a market capitalization of approximately $9 trillion. That is more than six times today's stock price. Executives receive nothing unless shareholders are extraordinarily well rewarded first.

Notably, Mark Zuckerberg is not part of the program. He doesn't need to be - he already owns roughly 13% of the company. His alignment with long-term shareholders is structural, not contractual.

This is not the behavior of a management team that believes it is building something temporary.

As in 2022, we believe the gap between price and value has widened. Our response is the same as it was then - stay focused on the business and let time do the work.

About Rowan Street Capital

Rowan Street Capital is a long-term, concentrated investment partnership founded in 2015 by Alex Kopel and Joe Maas. We are patient owners of a carefully selected group of exceptional businesses, investing alongside founder-operators and management teams who build durable value over time. We charge no fixed management fees - our compensation is tied directly to the performance of our partners' capital.

If our way of thinking resonates with you, we invite you to follow our work and explore our full investment philosophy at Rowan Street Capital.
