data.title: ICAP: Compelling Actively Managed Equity Strategy Facilitating Megatrend Exposure
data.description:
The Infrastructure Capital Equity Income ETF (ICAP) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund designed to maximize income and total returns by investing in dividend-paying equities and equity-like securities across all market capitalizations. To enhance growth and income, ICAP also writes call and put options, in part to hedge risk, enter or exit positions, and increase exposure. With its diversified approach to the markets, ICAP has the potential to provide alpha through dynamic positioning, an appealing approach that is oftentimes reserved for closed-end funds. Given its strong historical performance and transparent positioning, I recommend ICAP with a Buy rating.

Investment Thesis for ICAP

ICAP takes the broadest approach to creating its coverage universe, including everything from common stock, preferred shares, convertible notes, as well as options derivatives to create diversified exposure for investors. While the fund primarily invests in domestic securities, it does have the flexibility to regionally diversify through the use of ADRs, GDRs, and IDRs. In addition to this, the fund isn't limited to common stock, providing bandwidth to invest in real estate investment trusts [REITs] and master limited partnerships [MLPs], allowing the fund to diversify its investable universe into markets that may be less accessible to some investors and brokerage accounts due to tax reporting [K-1]. Accordingly, ICAP will issue a 1099 for tax reporting purposes, allowing for the fund to be utilized in tax-advantaged accounts.

The fund takes a hybrid approach to constructing the portfolio, starting at the top-down, top of the funnel with a macroeconomic view before building a bottom-up case for each individual security. For example, the top 3 holdings within the strategy include Marvell Technology (MRVL), Amazon (AMZN), and Citizens Financial Group (CFG).

Marvell Technology is one of the leading custom silicon and network silicon developers next to Broadcom (AVGO) in a market that is gradually turning towards XPUs for AI inferencing. Marvell recently announced that it is in discussions with Alphabet (GOOG) to develop two new chip designs, a memory processing unit and a new tensor processing unit. An agreement with Alphabet could deliver substantial growth for Marvell over the coming years as data center investments are expected to continue to expand to facilitate broader adoptions for enterprise and physical AI. Accordingly, more than $4T is expected to be invested towards computing hardware by 2030, a major upside potential for Marvell to participate in. MRVL shares account for 5.78% of the total portfolio weight.

Amazon is one of the leading hyperscalers developing gigawatts of data center capacity to drive growth for its cloud services customers. In a recent report covering the AMZN shares, I outlined the acquisition of Globalstar (GSAT) and how the satellite network may enhance and accelerate Amazon's internal physical AI adoption given Globalstar's 2-way data connection technology for industrial IoT. My theory is that Amazon may use this infrastructure to actively manage and monitor fully autonomous warehouses as well as final-mile delivery services in the coming years as AI-enabled robotics become more widely adopted. AMZN shares account for 5.27% of the total portfolio weight.

From a top-down perspective, ICAP presently favors electric utilities (8.39%), semiconductors (7.42%), and homebuilding (7%) as its top sector allocations. Some of the top holdings that fall into these categories include NextEra Energy (NEE) at 3.76%, Lennar Corporation (LEN) at 3.41%, and NVIDIA (NVDA) at 2.64%.

I believe that the fund is taking an appealing approach to the market given the growing demand for electricity driven by reindustrialization and data center developments. Utilities are expected to invest anywhere from $1.1T to $1.4T over the next 5 years to harden the grid, modernize infrastructure, and expand capacity. For reference, this is roughly equivalent to the level of investments over the last decade. While capital deployed will in part fall on the utilities, the growth aspect of the investments can deliver stronger cash flows in the coming years supported by market demand for electricity, potentially providing greater shareholder value and growth as opposed to maintaining the historical defensive label.

Overall, I believe the dynamic approach to constructing the strategy can be appealing to investors seeking a managed strategy without utilizing the CEF wrapper. The benefit to investors is that the fund is fully transparent in its holdings, allowing investors to understand what they own when buying shares of the ETF. With the convergence of sector rotation and fundamental security selection, I believe ICAP can continue to grow exceptionally well and deliver alpha to investors.

One factor that investors must consider with the fund is its options trading strategy. The fund will primarily sell call and put options to increase exposure to the market as well as to enter and exit positions. This can be an appealing component that can deliver income for the portfolio given the premium received from writing options derivatives. The downside risk is that the covered calls may limit the full upside potential of the equity securities within the strategy, potentially creating some drag on NAV. In addition to this, the fund may be exposed to certain risks regarding its written put options and be forced to purchase shares of a company if the stock were to undergo significant volatility. While the fund has performed exceptionally well when compared to the S&P 500 (SPX) and the Nasdaq-100 (NDX), these factors could create some headwinds during volatile trading periods.

About Infrastructure Capital Equity Income ETF

ICAP was launched by Infrastructure Capital Advisors on December 28, 2021, on the NYSE Arca Exchange. The strategy has a management fee of 80bps and a total expense ratio of 164bps. ICAP exhibits relatively thin liquidity with roughly $100mm in net assets, with an average of $753k in share value changing hands on a daily basis. As a result, ICAP has a relatively wide 30-day average bid/ask spread of 0.35%, potentially adding some costs when entering and exiting a position. Given that the strategy is actively managed, investors considering this fund will benefit from taking a buy-and-hold approach rather than actively trading the fund.

ICAP pays out a monthly distribution, which has exhibited growth to its regular monthly rate since inception. The fund paid out $2.62/share over the last twelve months for a yield of 9.10%.

Risks Related to ICAP

ICAP is an actively managed ETF designed to optimize total returns, exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. ICAP is actively managed, exposing investors to manager risk and the portfolio management team's ability to appropriately select sectors, securities, and security weights. The strategy also employs options derivatives, which in many cases are covered calls with some naked calls, as well as short put options to gain broader exposure to the market. Covered calls may cap performance to a certain extent, while naked call options may increase risk. In some cases, options derivatives are utilized to enter and exit positions, meaning that the covered calls and short put options are strategically utilized to gain exposure to a stock that the portfolio management team may have had the intent to purchase. Thin liquidity may add additional costs to the total ownership of the fund, though the fund should be considered for a buy-and-hold strategy rather than for active trading.

Final Thoughts

ICAP can provide investors with an appealing dynamic approach to investing in the broader market in an ETF wrapper. With active sector allocation and security selection, I believe ICAP is set up to generate returns for shareholders, particularly when considering the current exposure. I am recommending ICAP with a Buy rating for long-term investors.

data.title: Compass Minerals: Pittsburgh's Ice Reveals The Value Chain
data.description:
After this winter, we are likely to see pieces moving on the chessboard following the recent news surrounding Compass Minerals (CMP). To begin with, last January the company had accumulated a one-month delay in salt deliveries to around 100 communities in southwestern Pennsylvania, grouped under SHACOG.

In fact, the executive director of this consortium stated that the company did not seem prepared for a winter in western Pennsylvania. Perhaps those words did not sit well, and in a market operating with depleted inventories and demand through the roof, it was not worth renewing the contract. Or perhaps there was indeed an operational failure, and CMP has decided to use the opportunity to seek an exit.

What is clear is that the Great Lakes were partially frozen, with Lake Erie nearly 96% frozen in January, disrupting part of the salt shipments. And in a city like Cleveland, which entered that month with reserves below 10,000 tons, a single storm would have consumed around 4,000 tons or even 7,000 tons.

So now it is time to understand how CMP operates within the value chain and whether a situation like last winter could happen again.

An Abundant Mineral With A Tight Market

It seems ironic that there can be a shortage of one of the most abundant minerals in the world. With underground deposits and the oceans alone, salt reserves are practically inexhaustible, so the answer ends up being simple and obvious. There will be as much salt as long as the cost of extracting it and transporting it leaves profits for producers. Like everything else in economics.

Although the global market is huge, buyers usually seek the nearest producers, so it ends up being a regional market. For example, the price of rock salt used on roads is around $56 per ton at plants in the United States. From there, transportation alone can account for nearly 50% of the total cost.

Of this salt, approximately 41% in the United States goes to road deicing, while 39% goes to the chemical industry, since salt is necessary to produce chlorine and caustic soda. This last point is important to highlight because without this salt, chlorine cannot be produced, and without chlorine, PVC, disinfectants, or aluminum cannot be produced. Alternatives are still too expensive to remove salt from the equation.

What do I mean by all this? A company like CMP stands on the front line to keep these industries operating due to its business model. Further back in the chain, there is nothing left but geology. And because of its geographic position, CMP’s Goderich Mine in Ontario can reach markets such as Chicago, Detroit, Cleveland, Pittsburgh, Buffalo, Milwaukee, Minneapolis, Cincinnati, St. Louis, Toronto, and Montreal through the Great Lakes, with an advantage of roughly $5 to $10 per ton versus competitors relying on truck or rail.

And if we also consider that this product trades between $56 and $74 per ton, that advantage represents between 7% and 18% structurally. So I count two competitive advantages: first, a mine with a capacity of 9 million tons annually and reserves for 120 years; second, and more importantly, access to major end markets.

Position Of Power, Operational Failure, Or Both At The Same Time

So, going back to my initial question, was the SHACOG situation a natural event that was difficult to offset, an operational failure, or a position of power by Compass Minerals? To answer that question, I am going to lay out all the pieces of the puzzle in an attempt to reach a conclusion, just as I promised.

I begin with 2025, when Cargill sold all of its mines and exited the rock salt market. In the end, when one of the major players leaves the map, contracts are rising, as is the case in western New York by 35%, and logistical capacity is difficult to replicate, if not impossible. You have a scenario of power from the supply side if the new conditions are not attractive when the market is in your favor.

So was it an operational failure? For CMP, it was the first year in the Pittsburgh region. The salt leaves its mines and travels by barge to the RiverLift Industries depot in West Elizabeth for later truck distribution. At this point, it is unclear whether it was due to inventory or conditions, but starting on January 14, delays began, and in cities such as Scott Township, Castle Shannon, Kennedy Township, and Dormont, critical levels started to be reported.

Another cause is that the Allegheny River, Monongahela River, and Ohio River had between 3 and 6 inches of ice, which blocked barge traffic. So SHACOG pointed out that the company’s problem was failing to stock adequately to meet Pittsburgh demand before the freeze began, which later resulted in shipping disruptions.

Could it have been avoided then? I believe both sides are right, with the added factor that it is harder to assign blame when extreme events occur that, wrongly, are usually not planned for because they are considered less probable. In other words, according to SHACOG documentation, CMP did not meet the agreed supply before the rivers froze. But on the other hand, having Pittsburgh’s three rivers freeze simultaneously, while Lake Erie reached 96%, makes proper planning more difficult in my view.

How Much Does Losing SHACOG Really Hurt?

For this article, we have enough material to answer the question of what impact the SHACOG news will have. Pennsylvania consumes roughly 844,000 tons of road salt per year, and SHACOG groups around 100 municipalities in the southwest that represent between 8% and 12% of state consumption, meaning around 85,000 tons by my estimate.

CMP has guided for FY26 highway deicing volumes between 820,000 and 850,000 tons, so those 85,000 tons represent approximately 1% of the total. Therefore, I think the revenue impact is not as material as it may seem. With pricing at $73.96 per ton this past Q1 FY26, the loss equals around $6.3 million against total revenue of $1.3 billion.

I also believe this situation is already reflected in company guidance, since that outlook was issued in February, and the strategy management declared is to prioritize value over volume. That fits with losing SHACOG.

Moreover, my opinion is that if the needle moves, it looks more likely to move upward. If the 26-27 bid season materializes, for example, a 5% price increase would translate into roughly $12 million of additional EBITDA on its contract base, more than what it loses with SHACOG. So with the western New York contracts already mentioned, the Indiana RPF closing in May, and management commenting on low industry inventories, I see a structurally favorable setup for CMP.

In fact, results from the past Q1 showed sales growing 29% YoY to $396 million, with adjusted EBITDA of $65.3 million rising +103% YoY, and positive net income since 2023. So if we consider the stock price, trading around 8.5x forward EV/EBITDA, and although its average is higher than comparables, I also see a situation that justifies that premium, with the debt factor acting as a penalty. Otherwise, we would likely see an even higher multiple.

My Reading On CMP At These Prices

Could the same thing happen again this winter? Regarding salt, my opinion is yes. In other words, a mild winter could crush volumes as in previous years, while a harsher winter would send demand surging again.

But structurally, CMP’s position has changed considering that Cargill is out of the market. And if we add depleted inventories, while the first contracts of the bid season are already showing increases, I see the company in a stronger position.

In the next article on Compass Minerals, I will discuss its second business in greater depth, as it is also relevant, of course. The sulfate of potash produced from the Great Salt Lake, which is used by farmers in California for almond, strawberry, and pistachio production, has gone from operating losses of around $86 million in FY24 to positive FY26 guidance of $34 million.

I believe CMP is experiencing a business recovery, something also confirmed by the trend in the stock price. I think this is a favorable enough scenario for that trend to continue, with a better business than in the last three years, assets that are geographically irreplaceable, and a market backdrop that I believe is the most favorable in years.
